title: Chapter 22: The Prince's Summons
content: The shadow did not attack. It didn't need to. Its mere presence was an assault — on the senses, on the Pact, on the fundamental certainty that the sun belonged to Kael and nothing could touch him in its light.

Because the shadow stood in *direct sunlight* and didn't burn. It didn't even flicker. The noon heat passed through it, around it, and the darkness simply *ignored* the laws of physics that had killed every other vampire who'd dared step into the day.

Sera's blade was up, golden light blazing along its edge, but she didn't strike. Even through the transformation — even through the Pact-light that was turning her into something beyond human — her instincts were screaming. *Don't move. Don't breathe. Don't exist too loudly.*
The Prince's projection turned its furnace-eyes toward her.

"Ah. The anchor." The ancient voice carried something that might have been curiosity, if a ten-thousand-year-old vampire could still feel curiosity. "You're further along than my reports suggested. The golden veins. The luminous eyes. Do you know what you're becoming, child?"
Sera's grip on her blade tightened. "Do you?"

A sound emerged from the shadow — not laughter, exactly. More like the memory of laughter, filtered through millennia of accumulated indifference. "I know what the Blood Pact does to anchors who survive long enough. They don't. Not usually. The human vessel burns out within weeks — a year at most. The Pact's power is too much for mortal flesh. But you..." The shadow tilted its head. "You're not mortal anymore, are you? You're not quite anything. Which makes you either a miracle or a monster. I haven't decided which."

"Get to the point," Kael said. His voice was steady. The fear was there — he could feel it, cold and sharp, trying to freeze him in place — but fear was fuel if you knew how to burn it. "You didn't project yourself across half a continent to discuss Sera's medical condition."
"No." The Prince's attention shifted back to Kael, and the weight of it pressed down like a physical force. "I came to discuss yours."

The shadow moved. It walked — no, it *flowed* — across the balcony, past Kael, past Sera, and through the open doors into the war room. Kael and Sera followed. They had no choice. The shadow's presence was a gravitational pull, and resisting it would have meant admitting that resistance was possible.

The war room's torches dimmed as the shadow entered. The obsidian table reflected nothing — the darkness drank even that faint glimmer of light. The maps on the walls seemed to ripple, their ink bleeding into shapes that hadn't been drawn there.

"I felt Orsi die," Marius said. He was standing — if you could call it standing — at the head of the table, his shadow-form occupying the space where a physical body would have been. "Every vampire above Baron rank felt it. Count Alessio Orsi, who served the Tribunal for four hundred years. Who helped draft the Veil Doctrine. Who paid his blood-tithe on time and kept his territories quiet and never — not once — drew the attention of a Prince."
"He attacked me first."

"He did. And you killed him. You consumed his heart-blood and claimed his rank. All of which is perfectly legal under Tribunal law, provided the killing was done in honorable combat." The shadow paused. "Was it honorable combat, Count Ashworth?"

Kael met the furnace-eyes. "He ambushed me in a siege tunnel with thirty Barons and three Viscounts. I killed him with a sword through his heart in direct sunlight, with a paladin as my witness. If that's not honorable, your law needs rewriting."
"Bold words from a vampire who's been Count for less than a day."
"You didn't come here for bold words. You came here to threaten me. So threaten."

The shadow went very still. The torches flickered. Somewhere in Greyhaven, a vampire — a Baron, newly sworn to Kael's service — dropped to his knees in the street, clutching his chest, overwhelmed by a pressure that had no physical source. The weight of ten thousand years was pressing down on the entire city.
Then: the shadow smiled. There was no face to animate, no lips to curl. The smile was a *feeling* — cold and amused and terrifyingly patient.
"I like you. That's unfortunate. The ones I like tend to die badly."

"You are charged with three violations of Tribunal law," Marius said. The shadow's voice shifted — formal now, the cadence of an ancient court. "First: unsanctioned kills under the Blood Curse. You turned Marcus Thorne and thirty-seven others without Tribunal approval. Every one of those progeny carries a fragment of the Blood Curse in their veins. Every one of them is a potential Veil breach."
"Blood Curse?"

"The curse that makes us what we are, Count Ashworth. Did you think vampirism was a gift? It's a punishment. A judgment passed down so long ago that most of us have forgotten the crime. When you create progeny, you spread the Curse. The Tribunal has the right — the *duty* — to regulate that spread. You didn't ask permission. You didn't even send a letter."

Kael said nothing. He'd read the old texts. He knew the Curse existed. He also knew that no Prince had enforced it in living memory — it was a dead law, a relic, a clause that existed only to be used as a weapon when the Tribunal wanted to crush someone.

Marius continued. "Second: Veil Doctrine violation. The daylight immunity you've discovered — the Blood Pact anchor that lets you walk in the sun — is not supposed to exist. The Veil Doctrine was written to keep humanity ignorant of our world. A vampire who can walk in daylight is a *catastrophic* risk to that ignorance. Every human who sees you in the sun becomes a potential breach. Every report, every rumor, every story — they add up. How many humans have seen you, Count? How many know what you are?"
Kael thought of the old woman in the Ruins. The refugees at the Thorn Gate. The human quarter of Greyhaven, who'd watched him walk through noon sunlight with their own eyes.
"Enough," he admitted.

"Enough is too many. The Veil Doctrine doesn't have a threshold. It's absolute. One human is a breach. A city full of them..." The shadow's furnace-eyes flared brighter. "Is a declaration of war against the secrecy that has kept our species alive for ten millennia."
"And the third charge?"

The shadow's smile returned. Colder this time. "Illegal progeny army. You're building an army of daylight-immune soldiers. Not fifty. Not a hundred. You're building enough to challenge the Tribunal itself. Don't insult me by denying it."
"I'm not building an army to challenge the Tribunal. I'm building an army to protect my territory."

"Same thing. Every Count protects their territory. Every Duke. Every Prince. But none of us — *none* of us — have ever built a legion that can march at noon. The day belongs to humanity. That's the oldest rule we have. The one rule that even the Princes don't break. And you..." The shadow leaned forward, and the room got darker. "You broke it on your first day as a Viscount."
The silence that followed was absolute. Even the torches stopped crackling.

Kael's mind was racing. Three charges — all of them technically valid, all of them politically motivated, all of them carrying the same implicit threat: *We can crush you whenever we want. The only question is whether we'll do it legally first.*
"What do you want?" Kael asked.

"Want? I want you dead, Count Ashworth. I want the Blood Pact severed and your daylight immunity erased and every trace of your existence scrubbed from the records so thoroughly that no fledgling ever gets the same idea." The shadow shrugged — a gesture so human that it was jarring. "But I'm not a tyrant. I'm a Prince. I rule by law, not by whim. So I'm offering you a chance to answer these charges before the Tribunal."
"The Tribunal. At the Crimson Keep."
"You know of it. Good."
"I know it's where vampires go to die. I know that no Count has ever been summoned to the Tribunal and walked out alive."
"You'd be the first. You seem to enjoy being the first at things."
Kael's jaw tightened. "And if I refuse?"

"Then the Tribunal declares war. Not a coalition of Viscounts — a *real* war. Every Duke in the western territories. Every Prince who owes Marius fealty. An army that makes Orsi's coalition look like a border skirmish. They'll come during the day, Count Ashworth. Oh, not in the sunlight — they're not stupid. But they'll come through the deep tunnels, the ancient passages that run so far underground that sunlight has never touched them. They'll surround Greyhaven. They'll starve you out. They'll wait for a cloudy day, a stormy day, an eclipse — and then they'll burn everything you've built to ash."
The shadow straightened. The furnace-eyes burned into Kael's.

"Three days, Count Ashworth. You have three days to present yourself at the Crimson Keep and answer the charges. Failure to appear constitutes an admission of guilt. Punishment: death, territory forfeiture, and the complete eradication of the Polaris Guard. All progeny executed. All allies purged." A pause. "Including the anchor."

Sera's golden veins flared. The light from her skin pushed back against the shadow — not much, just a few inches, but enough to show that even a Prince's projection couldn't entirely suppress the Pact.
"That's not a trial," she said. "That's an execution with extra steps."

"That's Tribunal law." Marius's shadow began to recede, pulling back toward the walls, dissolving into the darkness it had come from. "Three days. Don't be late. I'd hate to send Duchess Valeria to collect you personally. She's not as patient as I am."
The shadow collapsed into a pool of darkness. The pool shrank. The torches flared back to full brightness. The pressure lifted.
And Kael and Sera were alone again in the war room, staring at the empty space where a ten-thousand-year-old Prince had just told them their death warrant was already signed.
Sera spoke first. "We're not going."
"We're going."
"Kael—"

"Refuse and he kills everyone. The Polaris Guard. Marcus. Every Baron who swore fealty. Every human in Greyhaven. *You*." He turned to face her, and his silver eyes were burning with something that wasn't quite fury and wasn't quite fear. "I didn't kill a Count and claim three territories just to watch it all burn because I was too proud to answer a summons."
"It's a trap."

"Obviously it's a trap. The question isn't whether it's a trap. The question is what kind." He started pacing, his new Count-speed making the motion blur at the edges. "If Marius wanted me dead, he'd have sent Valeria already. He wouldn't waste time with a tribunal. Summons. Legal charges. A *trial*." He stopped. "He's not trying to kill me. He's trying to control me."
"Same thing."
"Not the same thing. Killing is final. Control is a negotiation. A negotiation means there's something he wants."
Sera's golden eyes narrowed. "What could a ten-thousand-year-old Prince possibly want from a three-day-old Count?"

Kael didn't answer. But through the Pact, Sera felt the shape of his thoughts — a suspicion, half-formed, too dangerous to speak aloud. Something about the Blood Pact. Something about the daylight immunity Marius had called "not supposed to exist." Something about a Prince who'd lived ten thousand years and was still afraid of the sun.
"Well," Kael said finally, "I guess we're going to find out."
